## Real Material - Architect Learning Build (Data Engineering: Streaming Messaging)

This section is the study-first architecture guide for kinesis, kafka, msk, firehose, and flink streaming design.

### Phase 1 - Foundations

#### Module: Streaming Messaging Mental Model
- Use streaming messaging decisions to shape blast radius, access paths, and operational ownership.
- Study service behavior first, then map it into production architecture and exam-ready design language.

##### Transcript-Enriched Learning Notes
- Now, this Kafka topic is having real-time streaming of data and consumers will pull from the topic to consume the data itself and then your consumer can do whatever he wants, process it or send it to various destinations, such as EMR, S3, SageMaker, Kinesis and RDS.
- So to get data into Amazon Data Firehose, you may have producers that could be your applications, your clients, or things you write, and you use the SDK to send data into Firehose or the Kinesis agents, as well as Firehose has the capability to pull directly from some services such as, for example, a Kinesis Data Streams or an Amazon CloudWatch logs and events, or for example, AWS IoT.
- Apache Kafka is a way for you to stream data and a Kafka cluster is made of multiple brokers and then you will have producers that will produce data and so they will have to ingest data from places, such as Kinesis, IoT RDS, et cetera, et cetera, and they will send the data directly into a Kafka topic that is going to be fully replicated into other brokers.
- You can also send data directly into specified HTTP or HTTP(S) endpoints, but SNS also has integrations with specific AWS services, such as SQS, to send your message directly into a Queue, to Lambda, to have a function do some code after the message is received, or to Kinesis Data Firehose to send data into, for example, as Amazons free or Redshift.
- And in terms of security, you have IAM policies for authorization and authentication on the Kinesis Data Stream side and for security on Amazon MSK you can get Mutual TLS for authentication and Kafka ACLs for authorization that's combo, number one or SASL/SCRAM for authentication and Kafka ACLs for authorization.
- So if you see a large message in the exam this is going to rule out any cases that data streams answers or Firehose answers and go straight into Amazon MSK. then instead of the streams it's called Data Streams with Shards and in Amazon MSK it's called Kafka Topics with Partitions, but they're pretty similar.
- So this is our use case, or they can be Direct PUTs done through the Kinesis Data Agents, the Kinesis Agents, some other AWS services, such as CloudWatch, IoT Core, EventBridge, et cetera, et cetera, and also your own apps using the SDK that can send data directly into Kinesis Data Firehose.
- And then from Kinesis Data Firehose, you can send it into your Amazon S3 bucket, or for that matter, any supported KDF specific destination, which allows you to be really extensible in the way you want to maybe persist your messages from your SNS topic.
- In the previous lecture we created Simple Spark streaming job, which reads from the Kafka table, creates some aggregates windowing aggregates for 15 minute time window and saves that aggregate into a target table in the trade summary table.
- So you have Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink on the right hand side, and then you can read data from Kinesis Data Streams, or even Amazon MSK, which is Apache Kafka, which is also a service for real-time data streams.
- Because even though we have created the Firehose Delivery Stream, even if some data was sent in the past to the Kinesis Data Stream, you actually need to send new data after setting up Firehose for Firehose to be active.
- And so all these services are either going to be pushing into Amazon Data Firehose or Firehose are going to be pulling from, for example, a Kinesis data stream and it is going to receive the records.

- Treat Analytical Storage and Query as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.
- Treat Pipeline and Ingestion Design as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.
- Treat Streaming State and Time as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.
- Treat Consumption and BI as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.
- Treat Distributed Processing as a first-class architecture control, with explicit ownership and failure-mode design.
- Design implication: now, this Kafka topic is having real-time streaming of data and consumers will pull from the topic to consume the data itself and then your consumer can do whatever he wants, process it or send it to various destinations, such as EMR, S3, SageMaker, Kinesis and RDS.
- Design implication: so to get data into Amazon Data Firehose, you may have producers that could be your applications, your clients, or things you write, and you use the SDK to send data into Firehose or the Kinesis agents, as well as Firehose has the capability to pull directly from some services such as, for example, a Kinesis Data Streams or an Amazon CloudWatch logs and events, or for example, AWS IoT.
- Design implication: apache Kafka is a way for you to stream data and a Kafka cluster is made of multiple brokers and then you will have producers that will produce data and so they will have to ingest data from places, such as Kinesis, IoT RDS, et cetera, et cetera, and they will send the data directly into a Kafka topic that is going to be fully replicated into other brokers.
- Design implication: you can also send data directly into specified HTTP or HTTP(S) endpoints, but SNS also has integrations with specific AWS services, such as SQS, to send your message directly into a Queue, to Lambda, to have a function do some code after the message is received, or to Kinesis Data Firehose to send data into, for example, as Amazons free or Redshift.
- Design implication: and in terms of security, you have IAM policies for authorization and authentication on the Kinesis Data Stream side and for security on Amazon MSK you can get Mutual TLS for authentication and Kafka ACLs for authorization that's combo, number one or SASL/SCRAM for authentication and Kafka ACLs for authorization.

##### Transcript-Enriched Learning Notes
- So if you see a large message in the exam this is going to rule out any cases that data streams answers or Firehose answers and go straight into Amazon MSK. then instead of the streams it's called Data Streams with Shards and in Amazon MSK it's called Kafka Topics with Partitions, but they're pretty similar.
- So you have Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink on the right hand side, and then you can read data from Kinesis Data Streams, or even Amazon MSK, which is Apache Kafka, which is also a service for real-time data streams.
- And in terms of security, you have IAM policies for authorization and authentication on the Kinesis Data Stream side and for security on Amazon MSK you can get Mutual TLS for authentication and Kafka ACLs for authorization that's combo, number one or SASL/SCRAM for authentication and Kafka ACLs for authorization.
- You can use Lambda functions to directly have Amazon MSK as an event source or you can write your own Kafka consumer and you can make it run on whatever platform you want, for example, your Amazon EC2 instances, or an ECS cluster or an EKS cluster.
- And MSK is going to create and manage Kafka broker nodes and Zookeeper broker nodes in your cluster for you and you deploy the cluster in your VPC, across multiple AZ, up to three for high availability.
- And this is that you run Apache Kafka on MSK, but this time you don't provision servers, you don't manage capacity, automatically MSK will provision resources and scale, compute and storage for you.
- So, from personal experience, I know it's very difficult to set up Apache Kafka and the fact you can just do one click and then deploy Kafka on AWS is great and this is the Amazon MSK service.
- You can have Data Streams with Shards in Kinesis Data Streams or in MSK, it's called Kafka Topics with Partitions, but the concept are sort of similar.
- And as you should know, Flink can read from, yes, Kinesis Data Streams, but it cannot read from Amazon Data Firehose, and that could be an exam trick.
- So to produce to MSK you need to create a Kafka Producer and then to consume from MSK, you have multiple options.
- Another analytics service you will see is Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka, also called Amazon MSK.
- So you want a Flink Application and you make it read it directly from the MSK cluster.

#### Module: Operational and Architecture Patterns
- Patterns matter when scale, governance, or reliability turns basic service use into system design.
- Prefer design implications and trade-offs over memorizing isolated features.

##### Transcript-Enriched Learning Notes
- And in terms of security, you have IAM policies for authorization and authentication on the Kinesis Data Stream side and for security on Amazon MSK you can get Mutual TLS for authentication and Kafka ACLs for authorization that's combo, number one or SASL/SCRAM for authentication and Kafka ACLs for authorization.
- So you have Amazon Managed Service for Apache Flink on the right hand side, and then you can read data from Kinesis Data Streams, or even Amazon MSK, which is Apache Kafka, which is also a service for real-time data streams.
- So if you see a large message in the exam this is going to rule out any cases that data streams answers or Firehose answers and go straight into Amazon MSK. then instead of the streams it's called Data Streams with Shards and in Amazon MSK it's called Kafka Topics with Partitions, but they're pretty similar.
- Now, this Kafka topic is having real-time streaming of data and consumers will pull from the topic to consume the data itself and then your consumer can do whatever he wants, process it or send it to various destinations, such as EMR, S3, SageMaker, Kinesis and RDS.
- In the previous lecture we created Simple Spark streaming job, which reads from the Kafka table, creates some aggregates windowing aggregates for 15 minute time window and saves that aggregate into a target table in the trade summary table.
- Kafka streams library is designed with some ready made, ready to use methods and framework for developing stream processing applications to take care of many streaming challenges.
- And Spark Structured Streaming will use the watermark to do the state store cleanup when the window expired, right when window passed the watermark duration.
- You can have Data Streams with Shards in Kinesis Data Streams or in MSK, it's called Kafka Topics with Partitions, but the concept are sort of similar.
- And as you should know, Flink can read from, yes, Kinesis Data Streams, but it cannot read from Amazon Data Firehose, and that could be an exam trick.
- It's it's more of a, uh, event streaming solution or event driven architecture which follows microservice architecture pattern to design the solution.
- Another architecture is that you can send data directly from SNS to Amazon S3 through Kinesis Data Firehose.
- Another analytics service you will see is Amazon Managed Streaming for Apache Kafka, also called Amazon MSK.

### Phase 2 - Core Services
- Amazon Kinesis should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon MSK should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- AWS Config should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon Data Firehose should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.
- Amazon RDS should be understood in terms of request path, control plane, and operational boundary.

### Phase 3 - Advanced Patterns
- Pattern emphasis: Analytical Storage and Query becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Consumption and BI becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Data Engineering Operational Context becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Distributed Processing becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.
- Pattern emphasis: Lakehouse Governance becomes valuable when requirements move from feature use to system design discipline.

### Phase 4 - System Design (Production)
#### Reference Architecture
- Design streaming messaging with explicit service boundaries, operational controls, and recovery expectations.
#### Scaling Considerations
- Scaling risk appears when streaming messaging decisions are made service-by-service without a whole-system operating model.
#### Cost Considerations
- The main cost driver is usually misalignment between service choice, operations, and workload behavior.

## Hands-On Practice (Kid-Friendly Step-by-Step)

### Safety First
- Use a sandbox account only.
- Set a budget alert before creating resources.
- Delete resources after practice to avoid surprise cost.

### Hands-On Signals Found In This KB
- Signal 1: And MSK is going to create and manage Kafka broker nodes and Zookeeper broker nodes in your cluster for you and you deploy the cluster in your VPC, across multiple AZ, up to three for high availability.
- Signal 2: So, from personal experience, I know it's very difficult to set up Apache Kafka and the fact you can just do one click and then deploy Kafka on AWS is great and this is the Amazon MSK service.
- Signal 3: So to produce to MSK you need to create a Kafka Producer and then to consume from MSK, you have multiple options.
- Signal 4: Transcript signals that and MSK is going to create and manage Kafka broker nodes and Zookeeper broker nodes in your cluster for you and you deploy the cluster in your VPC, across multiple AZ, up to three for high availability.

### Service-Specific Lab Paths
### Activity 1: Amazon S3 Lab for Kinesis, Kafka, MSK, Firehose, and Flink streaming design
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> S3 -> Buckets
- Azure equivalent: Azure Blob Storage
- GCP equivalent: Cloud Storage
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> S3 -> Buckets.
3. Build: Create a bucket and upload one sample file.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Open object metadata and test download.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete objects and delete bucket.

### Activity 2: Amazon CloudWatch Lab for Kinesis, Kafka, MSK, Firehose, and Flink streaming design
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> CloudWatch
- Azure equivalent: Azure Monitor
- GCP equivalent: Cloud Monitoring
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> CloudWatch.
3. Build: Create one dashboard and one alarm.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Trigger alarm condition and inspect alarm history.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete dashboard and alarm.

### Activity 3: Amazon EC2 Lab for Kinesis, Kafka, MSK, Firehose, and Flink streaming design
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> EC2 -> Instances
- Azure equivalent: Azure Virtual Machines
- GCP equivalent: Compute Engine
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> EC2 -> Instances.
3. Build: Launch one small instance with default VPC.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Check instance running state and system status checks.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Terminate instance and remove extra volumes.

### Activity 4: Amazon DynamoDB Lab for Kinesis, Kafka, MSK, Firehose, and Flink streaming design
- AWS console path: AWS Console -> DynamoDB -> Tables
- Azure equivalent: Azure Cosmos DB
- GCP equivalent: Firestore
- Time: 12-20 minutes
1. Sign in to AWS console and set region.
2. Navigate: AWS Console -> DynamoDB -> Tables.
3. Build: Create a table with partition key and add one item.
4. Add tags: Project=Learning, Topic=KBPractice, Owner=Student.
5. Save and wait for status Ready or Active.
6. Verify: Query the item using the partition key.
7. Write one observation: what worked, what failed, what changed.
8. Cleanup: Delete test table.

### Quick Troubleshooting
- Access denied: verify role permissions or ask admin for least-privilege lab policy.
- Resource limit reached: pick another region or request quota increase.
- Unexpected charges: stop/delete resources and review cost explorer immediately.

## Cross-Cloud Thinking Mode

Use this lens to study Kinesis, Kafka, MSK, Firehose, and Flink streaming design in a cloud-agnostic way: focus on capability first, provider name second.

### Capability Mapping (AWS / Azure / GCP)
| Capability | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Identity | IAM / IAM Identity Center | Microsoft Entra ID + RBAC | Cloud IAM |
| Object Storage | Amazon S3 | Azure Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |
| Virtual Machines | Amazon EC2 | Azure Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| Serverless Functions | AWS Lambda | Azure Functions | Cloud Functions / Cloud Run |
| Containers | Amazon EKS / ECS | AKS | GKE / Cloud Run |
| Databases | RDS / DynamoDB / ElastiCache | Azure SQL / Cosmos DB / Azure Cache | Cloud SQL / Firestore / Memorystore |
| Networking | VPC / Route 53 / ELB | VNet / DNS / Load Balancer | VPC / Cloud DNS / Cloud Load Balancing |
| Observability | CloudWatch / X-Ray / CloudTrail | Azure Monitor / App Insights / Activity Log | Cloud Monitoring / Cloud Logging / Cloud Trace |

### Same Lab Across 3 Clouds
| Lab Theme | AWS | Azure | GCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Amazon S3 | Amazon S3 | Azure Blob Storage | Cloud Storage |
| Amazon CloudWatch | Amazon CloudWatch | Azure Monitor | Cloud Monitoring |
| Amazon EC2 | Amazon EC2 | Azure Virtual Machines | Compute Engine |
| Amazon DynamoDB | Amazon DynamoDB | Azure Cosmos DB | Firestore |

### How To Think Cross-Cloud
1. Start with workload requirement: latency, throughput, security, and compliance.
2. Map requirement to capability family (identity, network, compute, data, observability).
3. Compare service behavior, not branding: limits, scaling model, and failure modes.
4. Keep a portable architecture note: what is generic vs provider-specific.
5. Test same mini-lab in at least 2 clouds and write down differences.

### Child-Friendly Practice Challenge
- Pick one simple task (for example: upload one file, run one function, read one metric).
- Do the same task in AWS, Azure, and GCP.
- Fill this mini template:
  - What was the same?
  - What had a different name?
  - What felt easier/harder and why?

<!-- END PRACTICE_ENHANCEMENTS -->

## Cross-Domain Referrals

- No secondary referral sources were required for this topic.

### File: outputs/aws-certified-developer-associate-dva-c01/19_AWS Integration & Messaging_ SQS, SNS & Kinesis/219_SQS - Message Visibility Timeout.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now let's talk about an important concept, which is called the message visibility timeout.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So when a message is polled by a consumer, it becomes invisible to other consumers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's take an example.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We have the time going from left to right, and we have a consumer doing a ReceiveMessage request, and therefore, a message will be returned from the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now the visibility timeout begins.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And by default, that message visibility timeout is 30 seconds.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That means that during these 30 seconds, the message has to be processed, okay?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: And if you do so, that means that if the same or other consumers do a message request API call, then the message will not be returned.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Streaming State and Time
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And again another one during the timeout window, the message will not be returned.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So effectively, during the visibility timeout, that message is invisible to other consumers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: But after the visibility timeout is elapsed and if the message has not been deleted, then the message will be quote unquote put back into the queue and therefore, another consumer or the same consumer doing a receive message API call will receive the message again, the same message as before.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, this is something that's really important to understand.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As you can see, while we receive a message, it becomes invisible during the visibility timeout period.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Streaming State and Time
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: Now, if we take a look at the same diagram, what we notice is that if we don't process a message within the visibility timeout window, then it will be processed twice potentially, right?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: Because it will be received by two different consumers, or twice by the same consumer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Streaming State and Time
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so, if a consumer is actively processing a message but knows that it needs a bit more time to process the message because otherwise, it will go out of the visibility timeout window, there is an API called ChangeMessageVisibility.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so if a consumer knows that a message needs a bit more time to be processed and you don't want to process that message twice, then the consumer should call the ChangeMessageVisibility API to tell SQS, hey, do not make that message visible for now, okay?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I just need a bit more time to process this message.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so, how do you set this message visibility timeout?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well, if you set it to a really, really high value by default, say, hours, and the consumer crashes, then it will take hours until this message reappears, re-becomes visible in your SQS queue, and that will take a lot of time.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you set it to something really, really low, like a few seconds, what happens that if the consumer doesn't end up having enough time to process that message for whatever reason, then it will be read many times by different consumers and you may get duplicate processing.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Streaming State and Time
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the idea is that the visibility timeout should be set to something reasonable for your application and your consumer should be programmed that if they know they need a bit more time, then they should call the ChangeMessageVisibility API to get more time and increase the timeout of that visibility window.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 1:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now let's talk about Dead Letter Queues in SQS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The idea is that let's get into the scenario where a consumer fails to process a message within the visibility timeout period.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then we know that the message automatically goes back into the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the consumer reads the message, maybe there's a failure, maybe we don't have enough time.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The message goes back into the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, if that thing happens very often, that can be a problem.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For example, we read again the message, maybe there's something wrong with the message.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Maybe our consumer doesn't understand the message or can't process the message.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then the message will go back into the queue and it will happen again.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We'll read the message again from SQS and it will go again back into the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So we can set a threshold to how many times this can happen.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And, this failure loop can be a big problem, but we can set a MaximumReceives threshold.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And if that threshold is exceeded then we can tell SQS to say, "Well, this message looks a bit odd.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: It looks like it's being processed too many times and not successfully." Therefore, send this into a Dead Letter Queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And the Dead Letter Queue will contain that message and for later processing.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so the message will be removed from the first queue and sent into the second one.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Why do we have Dead Letter Queues?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well Dead Letter Queues are very useful for debugging and in case a message goes in Dead Letter Queue because it is an SQS queue, you have to process it, but at least it gives you time to understand what is happening.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Few things to notice that the Dead Letter Queue of a FIFO queue must also be a FIFO queue, and the Dead Letter Queue of the Standard queue must also be a Standard queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Finally, because we have a Dead Letter Queue, you need to make sure that the messages are processed before They expire from the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So it's a good idea to set a long time for example, 14 days of retention from within the Dead Letter Queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And the next feature for management of your Dead Letter Queues are the re drive to source feature.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So it's a feature to help you consume messages in the Dead Letter Queue to understand what is wrong with them.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you have your messages now you know they haven't been processed in the source queue and therefore they are in the Dead Letter Queue and you're going to do a manual inspection and debugging of these messages.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And then you're going to fix your consumer code understand why the message wasn't processed in case the message was correct.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And then what you can do is re drive that message from the dead letter queue into the source SQS queue And what's going to happen with this is that the consumer can now reprocess that message without even knowing that the message went into the Dead Letter Queue and then the message processing has happened and as a cool feature.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now let us go into the console so I can show you the Dead Letter Queue feature.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

### File: outputs/aws-certified-developer-associate-dva-c01/19_AWS Integration & Messaging_ SQS, SNS & Kinesis/223_SQS - Certified Developer concepts.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now let's talk about some more concepts you need to know for SQS, but more at the developer level.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the first one is called long polling.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So when a consumer requests a message from SQS, it has the option to quote, unquote wait for messages to arrive if somehow the queue is empty.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And this is called long polling.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's take an example.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We have an SQS queue and it is empty and the consumer does a poll request into the SQS queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now we have the option to wait and it's fine to wait, right?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Because there are no messages.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So that means that if during the waiting period a message goes into the SQS queue, then the message will be received by the consumer then.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So why do we do long polling?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well, we do long polling because we are doing less API calls into the SQS queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And on top of it, we know that as soon as the message will arrive in the SQS queue, then the SQS queue will send it back to the consumer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So we are increasing the efficiency because we do less API calls.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon ECR
- Key Insights: So less CPU cycles are used and we are also decreasing the latency because as soon as a message will be received by your SQS queue, it will be received by your consumer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So long polling is a no-brainer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Long polling, you can set between 1 second to 20 seconds, but 20 seconds is preferable, why not?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And overall, it is a recommendation in your applications to use long polling to short polling.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you will see in the exam some questions maybe telling you that the consumer is doing too many calls into the SQS queue, and that is costing you money and CPU cycles, and maybe increasing the latency.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Then long polling is going to be the option.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 21:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 22:

- Services: (none explicit)

Line 23:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 24:

- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

Line 26:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon S3

Line 27:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So the second example, the producer wants to send a large message into SQS, but first what's going to happen is that the actual large message will end up in Amazon S3, and what will be sent into your SQS queue will be a small metadata message that has a pointer to the larger message in your Amazon S3 buckets.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So the SQS queue will contain small messages and your Amazon S3 bucket will contain large objects.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And your consumer, when it reads from the SQS queue, using this library, the SQS extended clients, then it will consume this small metadata message, which will say to the consumer, "Hey, go read that bigger message out of Amazon S3, and the consumer will be able to read and retrieve large messages from S3.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So a typical use case for this is if you're processing video files, you don't send the entire video file into your SQS queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: No, you upload that video file into your Amazon S3 bucket, and you send a small message with a pointer to that video file into your SQS queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And that allows you to accommodate any message size really through this pattern.

Line 39:

Line 40:

Line 44:

### File: outputs/aws-certified-developer-associate-dva-c01/19_AWS Integration & Messaging_ SQS, SNS & Kinesis/224_SQS - FIFO Queues.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now let's talk about Amazon's SQS FIFO queues.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So FIFO means first in, first out, and it corresponds to the ordering of messages in the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: That means that when you have a producer that send messages in a specific order, then one, two, three, four, then the consumer's polling messages from your SQS FIFO queue is going to read these messages in the same order.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is a guarantee of the FIFO queue but this is not true for just a regular SQS queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Messages can be received out of order.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design
- Services: AWS Batch
- Key Insights: So because you have this ordering guarantee, you have limits on throughput, about 300 messages per second without batching, and 3,000 messages per second with batching.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Dataflow and pipeline signal; Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: FIFO queue also have the ability to do something called exactly-once send.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That means that any duplicates can be removed directly at the queue level.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For this, you're just going to provide on every message you send a deduplication ID.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And if this ID is seen twice in the time range of five minutes, then duplicates are going to be removed.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is a very handy thing if you want to make sure you have no duplicates within five minutes.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The messages are going to be processed in order by the consumer and the ordering guarantee is at the message group ID level.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

### File: outputs/aws-certified-developer-associate-dva-c01/19_AWS Integration & Messaging_ SQS, SNS & Kinesis/227_Amazon SNS and SQS - Fan Out Pattern.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now let's talk about the SNS plus SQS fan-out pattern.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The idea is that you want a message to be sent to multiple SQS queues, but if you send them individually to every SQS queue, there can be problems associated with it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For example, if your application crashes in between, if there are delivery failures, or if you add more SQS queues down the road.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Therefore we want to use the fan-out pattern.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The idea is that you're going to push once an SNS topic and then you're going to subscribe as many SQS queues as you want to the SNS topic.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: These queues are subscribers and they will all receive the messages sent into SNS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So for example, we have a buying service that wants to send messages to two SQS queues.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: What it will do instead, it will send one message into an SNS topic, and the queues are subscribers of that SNS topic so that the fraud service and the shipping service can read all messages from their own SQS queues.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The idea with this as well is that it's a fully decoupled model and there is no data loss.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: SQS will give you data persistence, delayed processing, as well as retries of work.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And with this pattern, we can add more SQS queues as a subscriber of the SNS topic over time.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For this, we need to make sure that your SQS queue access policy, as we've seen from before, allows your SNS topic to write to your SQS queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So again, yet another use case for using queue access policies, and we have cross-region delivery.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So it's definitely possible for an SNS topic in one region to send messages to SQS queues in other regions if the security allows it.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Next, so how do we use this pattern for other purposes?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: For example, S3 events into multiple queues.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So there is a limitation of S3 event rules is that for a combination of event type, for example, an object is being created and a prefix, for example, images/, you can only have one S3 event rule.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: But what if you want to send the same S3 event notification to multiple SQS queues?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In that case, you would use the fan-out pattern.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So for example, we have S3 object created as an event appearing in your S3 bucket, and we will send this event into an SNS topic, and we will subscribe many SQS queues to the SNS topic as a final pattern.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS Lambda
- Key Insights: But we could also subscribe other type of application, email, Lambda functions, et cetera, et cetera.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And then what we get from this is that the message of the event happening in Amazon S3 is going to go to many different destinations thanks to this fan-out pattern.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Streaming State and Time
- Services: Amazon Data Firehose, Amazon Kinesis, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: Another architecture is that you can send data directly from SNS to Amazon S3 through Kinesis Data Firehose.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So because SNS has a direct integration with KDF, then your buying service can send data into an SNS topic.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 25:
- Concepts: Streaming State and Time
- Services: Amazon Data Firehose, Amazon Kinesis
- Key Insights: Then Kinesis Data Firehose, KDF, will receive that information.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 26:
- Concepts: Streaming State and Time
- Services: Amazon Data Firehose, Amazon Kinesis, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And then from Kinesis Data Firehose, you can send it into your Amazon S3 bucket, or for that matter, any supported KDF specific destination, which allows you to be really extensible in the way you want to maybe persist your messages from your SNS topic.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 27:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So we can apply the fan-out pattern to FIFO topics as well.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 28:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So Amazon SNS has a FIFO or FIFO capability, which is first in first out, which gives ordering of messages in the topic.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 29:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the producer send the messages.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 30:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: One, two, three, four.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 31:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And the subscribers can only be an SQS FIFO queue for now, which is receiving messages, one, two, three, four, in order.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal.

Line 32:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: So the idea is that with SNS FIFO, we get the same features as SQS FIFO, we get ordering by message group ID.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 33:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We get deduplication using a deduplication ID or content-based deduplication.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 34:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And both SQS standard and FIFO queues can be subscribers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 35:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: In terms of throughput, you're limited.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 36:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: You get the same throughput as the SQS FIFO queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 37:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So why do we need this?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well, in case you wanna do a fan out using SQS FIFO.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 39:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you need fan out, ordering and deduplication.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 40:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the buying service will send the data into an SNS FIFO topic, and then it will fan out to two SQS FIFO queue, which can have the fraud service and the shipping service read from FIFO queues as well.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 41:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: One last feature of SNS that can be really handy regarding the fan-out pattern is that you can do message filtering in SNS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 43:

Line 44:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 45:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 46:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 47:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 48:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 49:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So for this, we will subscribe the SQS queue into the SNS topic, and we'll apply a filter policy in JSON, and we will specify in a policy that we want to have State equals Placed.

Line 50:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)

Line 51:

Line 52:

Line 53:

- Key Insights: So, if you want to write what I call an optimized producer application that is meant for high throughput, you should be using the Kinesis Producer Library, KPL.

Line 36:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So, that means you get one megabyte per second, or a thousand records per second for each shard in terms of capacity.

Line 37:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And each shard in terms of read capacity now, in terms of out-traffic is two megabytes per second.

Line 38:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So, if you want to send, for example, 10,000 records per second, or 10 megabytes per second, then you will need to scale to 10 shards.

Line 40:

- Services: Amazon RDS

Line 41:

- Services: (none explicit)

- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

Line 2:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon Data Firehose
- Key Insights: So Amazon Data Firehose is a service to send data from sources into target destinations.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Pipeline and Ingestion Design, Streaming State and Time
- Services: Amazon CloudWatch, Amazon Data Firehose, Amazon Kinesis
- Key Insights: So to get data into Amazon Data Firehose, you may have producers that could be your applications, your clients, or things you write, and you use the SDK to send data into Firehose or the Kinesis agents, as well as Firehose has the capability to pull directly from some services such as, for example, a Kinesis Data Streams or an Amazon CloudWatch logs and events, or for example, AWS IoT.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Dataflow and pipeline signal.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design, Streaming State and Time
- Services: Amazon Data Firehose, Amazon Kinesis, Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: And so all these services are either going to be pushing into Amazon Data Firehose or Firehose are going to be pulling from, for example, a Kinesis data stream and it is going to receive the records.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Dataflow and pipeline signal.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS Lambda, Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: Now these records are going to be received, and then they can optionally be transformed using a Lambda function, if you wanted to do some data conversion format, for example.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design
- Services: AWS Batch
- Key Insights: And they're going to be accumulated into a buffer and the buffer is going to be flushed once in a while to do batch writing into a lot of destinations.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Analytical Storage and Query, Pipeline and Ingestion Design
- Services: Amazon OpenSearch, Amazon Redshift, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So the first destinations are going to be AWS specific destinations, such as sending data into Amazon S3 or into Amazon Redshift to perform analytics, or into Amazon OpenSearch as well.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

Line 49:
- Concepts: Streaming State and Time
- Services: Amazon Data Firehose, Amazon Kinesis, Amazon RDS
- Key Insights: So the buffer is a way for Kinesis Data Firehose to accumulate records before delivering them into the targets.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 50:
- Concepts: Streaming State and Time
- Services: Amazon Kinesis, Amazon S3
- Key Insights: And so by default, Kinesis will write five megabytes of data into the buffer before delivering it into the target, so Amazon S3.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 51:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now, you can make the buffer more efficient, for example more than 128 megabytes, if you wanted to get a bigger buffer size and more efficiency, or a small buffer size, if you wanted to deliver the data as fast as possible.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 52:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So we'll have it as 1.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 53:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And then, the buffer interval, so this is how fast, if the buffer size doesn't fill up.

Line 54:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: How fast should it be flushed into the target?

Line 55:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so, if you choose 300 seconds, you're going to wait five minutes to fill the buffer size.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 56:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But if the buffer size is not filled after five minutes, then it's going to be flushed nonetheless.

Line 57:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon S3
- Key Insights: So if we set a lower buffer interval, such as 60 seconds, we have the guarantee that at maximum, every 60 seconds, the buffer is going to be flushed into Amazon S3.

Line 58:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context

### File: outputs/aws-certified-solutions-architect-associate-saa-c03/17_Decoupling applications_ SQS, SNS, Kinesis, Active MQ/185_SQS - Message Visibility Timeout.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now let's talk about an important concept, which is called the message visibility timeout.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So when a message is polled by a consumer, it becomes invisible to other consumers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's take an example.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: We have the time going from left to right, and we have a consumer doing a ReceiveMessage request, and therefore, a message will be returned from the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Now the visibility timeout begins.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And by default, that message visibility timeout is 30 seconds.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That means that during these 30 seconds, the message has to be processed, okay?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: And if you do so, that means that if the same or other consumers do a message request API call, then the message will not be returned.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Streaming State and Time
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And again another one during the timeout window, the message will not be returned.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So effectively, during the visibility timeout, that message is invisible to other consumers.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: But after the visibility timeout is elapsed and if the message has not been deleted, then the message will be quote unquote put back into the queue and therefore, another consumer or the same consumer doing a receive message API call will receive the message again, the same message as before.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, this is something that's really important to understand.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: As you can see, while we receive a message, it becomes invisible during the visibility timeout period.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Streaming State and Time
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: Now, if we take a look at the same diagram, what we notice is that if we don't process a message within the visibility timeout window, then it will be processed twice potentially, right?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: Because it will be received by two different consumers, or twice by the same consumer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 16:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Streaming State and Time
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so, if a consumer is actively processing a message but knows that it needs a bit more time to process the message because otherwise, it will go out of the visibility timeout window, there is an API called ChangeMessageVisibility.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 17:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so if a consumer knows that a message needs a bit more time to be processed and you don't want to process that message twice, then the consumer should call the ChangeMessageVisibility API to tell SQS, hey, do not make that message visible for now, okay?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 18:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: I just need a bit more time to process this message.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 19:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so, how do you set this message visibility timeout?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 20:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well, if you set it to a really, really high value by default, say, hours, and the consumer crashes, then it will take hours until this message reappears, re-becomes visible in your SQS queue, and that will take a lot of time.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 21:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: If you set it to something really, really low, like a few seconds, what happens that if the consumer doesn't end up having enough time to process that message for whatever reason, then it will be read many times by different consumers and you may get duplicate processing.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 22:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI, Streaming State and Time
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the idea is that the visibility timeout should be set to something reasonable for your application and your consumer should be programmed that if they know they need a bit more time, then they should call the ChangeMessageVisibility API to get more time and increase the timeout of that visibility window.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal; Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 23:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: But understanding this concept is very important from an exam perspective because there will be scenarios on that.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 24:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, let's go in the console to see how that works in practice.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 27:

### File: outputs/aws-certified-solutions-architect-associate-saa-c03/17_Decoupling applications_ SQS, SNS, Kinesis, Active MQ/186_SQS - Long Polling.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So let's go ahead and discuss long polling in Amazon SQS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the idea that your consumer, that is requesting messages from the queue, and it can wait for the messages to arrive, if, when you do a request, there are no messages for now in the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: This is called long polling.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you have a queue and then your consumer is going to poll the queue, but the consumer can wait for a message to arrive.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So a message is going to arrive.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And then when, as soon as the message arrives, it's going to be sent to the consumer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: Amazon ECR
- Key Insights: So the idea is that with the long polling, your consumer is going to decrease the number of API calls, because they are long API calls.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Your consumer is asking SQS Queue maybe for 10 seconds for a message.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And also, while you have less API calls, you're going to also reduce the latency of your application.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Why?
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Well, because as soon as a message is received by SQS, it is going to be sent to the consumer.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And so therefore your consumer will receive your messages with the least amount of lag.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So the long polling can be set anywhere between one second to 20 seconds, and obviously, the longer you wait, the more efficient you're going to do and the less API calls you're going to do to SQS.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 14:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So, a wait time of 20 seconds may be preferable.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 15:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So overall you should prefer long polling to short polling, and you can enable this at the queue level or at the API level using the WaitTimeSeconds parameters.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Operational guidance / best-practice signal.

Line 16:

- Services: (none explicit)

### File: outputs/aws-certified-solutions-architect-associate-saa-c03/17_Decoupling applications_ SQS, SNS, Kinesis, Active MQ/187_SQS - FIFO Queues.txt

Line 1:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So now let's talk about Amazon's SQS FIFO queues.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 2:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So FIFO means first in, first out, and it corresponds to the ordering of messages in the queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 3:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: AWS SAM
- Key Insights: That means that when you have a producer that send messages in a specific order, then one, two, three, four, then the consumer's polling messages from your SQS FIFO queue is going to read these messages in the same order.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 4:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is a guarantee of the FIFO queue but this is not true for just a regular SQS queue.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 5:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: Messages can be received out of order.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 6:
- Concepts: Pipeline and Ingestion Design
- Services: AWS Batch
- Key Insights: So because you have this ordering guarantee, you have limits on throughput, about 300 messages per second without batching, and 3,000 messages per second with batching.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Constraint or limitation signal; Dataflow and pipeline signal; Distributed state and scaling signal.

Line 7:
- Concepts: Consumption and BI
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: FIFO queue also have the ability to do something called exactly-once send.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 8:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: That means that any duplicates can be removed directly at the queue level.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 9:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: For this, you're just going to provide on every message you send a deduplication ID.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 10:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: And if this ID is seen twice in the time range of five minutes, then duplicates are going to be removed.

Line 11:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So this is a very handy thing if you want to make sure you have no duplicates within five minutes.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 12:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: The messages are going to be processed in order by the consumer and the ordering guarantee is at the message group ID level.
- Hidden/Implicit Meaning: Implicitly contributes data engineering behavior detail.

Line 13:
- Concepts: Data Engineering Operational Context
- Services: (none explicit)
- Key Insights: So you need to send a message group ID every time you send a message to Amazon SQS FIFO queue, and then all the messages are going to be in order for that specific group ID.

Line 8:
